coredata.dc:title: Facial precipitation fabrication of visible light driven nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated iodine bismuth oxide catalysts
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xueling
Guo, Xiya
Song, Jinling
Han, Pei
Xin, Guoxiang
Wang, Ruifen
coredata.dc:description: A series of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated iodine bismuth oxide (BiOI/x%N-GQDs, x = 0,1,3,5,7,9,10) was prepared by a facial precipitation method at room temperature. Several characterizations indicated that 1–9 nm N-GQDs were evenly decorated on the surface of BiOI nanosheets. The visible light driven photocatalytic experiments showed that BiOI/9%N-GQDs possessed an excellent degradation property towards 20 mg/L methyl orange (MO), and its degradation efficiency obtained 93.11%. The results of free radical capture experiments demonstrated that h+ played a major role during the process of degrading MO. The possible photocatalytic mechanism had been proposed. Besides, BiOI/9%N-GQDs catalyst effectively degraded other organic pollutants, such as rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), bisphenol A (BPA), and inorganic pollutant of Cr(Ⅵ). The catalytic property of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was better than those of the similar reported systems. The catalytic system of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was predicted to have good prospects in further practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
N-GQDs
BiOI
Pollutants
Precipitation method

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images of N-GQDs, (b) XPS survey of N-GQDs, Inset, N1s, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs, inset, images of N-GQDs aqueous solution under sun and 356 nm ultraviolet light irradiation and (d) PL spectra of N-GQDs excited at different excitation wavelength.
XRD patterns of N-GQDs and BiOI/x%N-GQDs.
XPS spectra of BiOI/9%N-GQDs, (a) survey scan, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) I 3d, (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s.
(a, b) SEM images, (c) EDS and (d) mapping image of BiOI, (e, f) SEM images, (g) EDS and (h) mapping image of BiOI/9%N-GQDs.
TEM and HRTEM images of BiOI/9%N-GQDs.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of 20 mg/L MO, (b) adsorption and photocatalytic degradation efficiency and (c) the corresponding kinetics curves in the present of BiOI/x%N-GQDs under visible-light irradiation, and (d) the degradation spectra of MO in the present of BiOI/9%N-GQDs.

(a) UV–Vis DRS spectra, (b) the band gaps, (c) the intensity changes of the emission peak around 470 nm of BiOI/x%N-GQDs under 315 nm excitation, and (d) photocurrent response of the BiOI and BiOI/9%N-GQDs.

(a) Trapping experiments of the active species during photocatalytic degradation of MO, and (b) PL spectral changes, inset, comparison of PL peak intensity against irradiation time in the present of BiOI/9%N-GQDs.
Scheme of photo-degradation mechanism for BiOI/9%N-GQDs under visible light.
Photocatalytic degrading (a) 20 mg/L RhB, (b) 20 mg/L MB, (c) 20 mg/L BPA and (d) 15 mg/L Cr(Ⅵ) in the present of the BiOI/9%N-GQDs under visible light.

head.ce:title: Facial precipitation fabrication of visible light driven nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated iodine bismuth oxide catalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material and Metallurgy, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou, Inner Mongolia 014010, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Metallurgy, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material and Metallurgy, Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology, Baotou, Inner Mongolia 014010, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated iodine bismuth oxide (BiOI/x%N-GQDs, x = 0,1,3,5,7,9,10) was prepared by a facial precipitation method at room temperature. Several characterizations indicated that 1–9 nm N-GQDs were evenly decorated on the surface of BiOI nanosheets. The visible light driven photocatalytic experiments showed that BiOI/9%N-GQDs possessed an excellent degradation property towards 20 mg/L methyl orange (MO), and its degradation efficiency obtained 93.11%. The results of free radical capture experiments demonstrated that h+ played a major role during the process of degrading MO. The possible photocatalytic mechanism had been proposed. Besides, BiOI/9%N-GQDs catalyst effectively degraded other organic pollutants, such as rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), bisphenol A (BPA), and inorganic pollutant of Cr(Ⅵ). The catalytic property of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was better than those of the similar reported systems. The catalytic system of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was predicted to have good prospects in further practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the increasingly water shortage and water pollution have seriously constrained social and economic sustainable development [1]. Photocatalytic technology has been considered to be one of the most promising wastewater treatment technology [2–6]. However, the key of the photocatalytic technology is the used catalysts. Bismuth oxyhalide (BiOX, X = Cl, Br, I) has been attracted massive attention due to its unique layered structure, the excellent electrical and optical properties [7,8]. Among them, Iodine bismuth oxide (BiOI) could be a more hopeful visible light driven photocatalyst [9–13]. However, the narrow band gap (1.7–1.9 eV) of BiOI makes photogenerated electrons and holes easily recombine and further exhibit low photocatalytic efficiency. Constructing composites can promote the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [14–16].

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can build the surface separation centers and improve the separation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [17–23]. Some GQDs-semiconductors composites such as GQDs/CeO2 [24], GQDs/BWO [25], TiO2/GQDs [26], GQDs/PCN [27], GQDs/Sb2WO6 [28] have been prepared and improved their properties. Moreover, doping nitrogen to GQDs can efficiently induce charge delocalization, tune the work function of carbon and prompt the electron transfer capability of GQDs [29]. Based on the above-analysis, the combination of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and BiOX can improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. For example, Yin et al. [30] prepared the BiOBr and N-GQDs composites by a one-pot solvothermal method. The degradation efficiency of BiOBr/N-GQDs composites towards rhodamine B (RhB) was 3 times that of the pure BiOBr. Mou et al. [31] fabricated BiOCl/N-GQDs composites by one-step hydrothermal process. Compared to pure BiOCl, BiOCl/N-GQDs composites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic efficiency for antibiotic ciprofloxacin (CIP). As well as we known, there are not relative reports about BiOI/N-GQDs composites, especially BiOI/N-GQDs fabricated through room temperature precipitation method. Additionally, our earlier experimental results indicated that the photocatalytic properties of BiOXs prepared by facial precipitation are better than that obtained by solvothermal method [32].

In view of these results, a series of BiOI/x%N-GQDs photocatalysts was prepared by facial chemical precipitation at room temperature. During the precipitation reaction process, tetrapropylammonium iodide was used as an iodine source to control the release rate of I-, further tuning the growth morphology of the catalysts. BiOI/x%N-GQDs (x = 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10) were used to degrade simulated pollutant methyl orange (MO) to evaluate the photocatalytic performance, so as to determine the optimal ratio and explore the photocatalytic mechanism.

The N-GQDs were assembled by thermal decomposition process. 25 mL H2O with 0.8 g ammonium citrate (AC) was heated to 180 °C using an oil bath for 40 min. During the reaction process, the color of the mixed liquid was varied from colorless to orange. Then, the orange solution was cooled to room temperature, adjusted the pH value to 7 with 10 M NaOH solution, and collected to store at 4 °C for further use.

0.006 mol of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O was put into the solution A with 40 mL ethylene glycol and 0.5 mL lactic acid. Subsequently, 0.003 mol of tetrapropylammonium iodide was dispersed in solution B with 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 20 mL of deionized water. The solution A and B were sonicated for 10 min and then stirred for 30 min, respectively. The solution B was dropwise added into the solution A with continuous stirring. The obtained mixture solution was stirred for 30 min. The resultant precipitate was centrifuged, washed with deionized water, and then dried at 80 °C for 6 h. Finally, the powder was obtained by grinding, and noted as BiOI.

BiOI/x%N-GQDs (x = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10) were prepared by the above similar process, in which x means the mass ratio of AC to BiOI. A certain proportion of the prepared N-GQDs solution was added to solution B. The other preparation processes were the same as those of BiOI.

The crystal phases of samples were measured by X‐ray diffraction (XRD, PW1700). The measure condition was 4°/min at the range of 20–80° under the radiation source of CuKα. The morphology of the samples was recorded on the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU8200) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G20). The chemical composition analysis was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The functional group information was obtained by Raman spectra (Horiva, HPLORAPLUS). The optical properties were measured by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS, U-3900), Photoluminescence (PL, F-4600) and a CHI600E electrochemical station.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples was tested by our reported method [33]. Differently, 20 mg/L methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB), bisphenol A(BPA) and 15 mg/L Cr(Ⅵ) were selected as the simulated pollutants.
The separation efficiency of charge carriers was tested by an electrochemical workstation with a typical three-electrode system. The condition was the same as the reported paper [34].

In order to confirm the formation of N-GQDs, several measurements have been proceeded and the relative results were showed in the Fig. 1. According to the TEM image of N-GQDs in Fig. 1a, N-GQDs exhibited a spherical-like shape with a diameter of about 1–9 nm. It could be seen from the HRTEM (the inset of Fig. 1a) image that a lattice spacing of N-GQDs was 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (11
2 ¯
0) lattice fringes of graphene [35,36]. As shown in Fig. 1b, the full range XPS spectra of N-GQDs contained carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements, the atomic percentages of which were about 49.75%, 9.39%, 40.86%, respectively. From the inset of Fig. 1b, the N1s XPS also had four predominant peaks at 399.1, 399.7, 400.5 and 401.5 eV (Fig. 1c), attributing to pyridinic type, pyrrolic type, graphitic type and NH bonds, respectively [37]. And the contents of graphitic N, pyrrolic N and the pyridinic N were 18.54%, 37.31% and 25.58%. The C1s spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. S1) presented five peaks located at 284.8, 285.1, 286.7, 288.0 and 288. 8 eV, which were attributed to CC, CN, CN, CO, and CO, respectively [38].

Optical properties of the N-GQDs sample were characterized by UV–vis adsorption spectrum (Fig. 1c) and PL (Fig. 1d). In Fig. 1c, it could be observed that the characteristic ultraviolet absorption peak of N-GQDs was located at about 330 nm. This might be caused by the electron transition from π orbit to π* orbit in CC [39]. The inset photographs of Fig. 1c showed that the color of N-GQDs liquid was light orange and N-GQDs aqueous solution could not emit light under the sun light irradiation, while it emitted a uniform blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm UV light, indicating good dispersibility of N-GQDs in aqueous solution. From Fig. 1d, under the excitation of the 350–380 nm light, the corresponding maximum emission wavelength was all around 440 nm. Compared with the reported GQDs (about 458 nm), the emission wavelength of N-GQDs was blue-shift, which could be attributed from the strong electron affinity of N atoms doped in the N-GQDs [36,40]. All the results demonstrated that the N-GQDs were successfully prepared by thermal decomposition of AC.

Fig. 2 showed that the XRD patterns of the N-GQDs and BiOI/x%N-GQDs. A wide diffraction peak at 30.06° was displayed in the spectrum of N-GQDs, which belonged to (002) plane of graphene. And the diffraction peak was wider, indicating that the size of the formed grain was smaller and N-GQDs possessed the features of less graphene. For BiOI/x%N-GQD, they can well be indexed to the tetragonal BiOI (JCPDS No. 10-0445). The diffraction peaks at 9.66°, 29.65°, 31.66°, 37.39°, 39.36°, 45.37°, 51.34° and 55.15° were assigned to the (001), (102), (110), (112), (004), (200), (114) and (212) planes, respectively. It can be seen that there was no characteristic peak of N-GQD in the diffraction patterns of composites, which may be caused by the small amount of N-GQDs.

In order to further analyze the chemical components of composites, XPS spectra of BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite were carried out. The survey XPS spectrum of the BiOI/9%N-GQDs showed the peaks of Bi, O, I, N, and C in the Fig. 3a. In the Bi4f spectrum (Fig. 3b), the peaks located at 158.22 and 163.55 eV were ascribed to Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2, respectively, which illustrated the Bi element existed in the form of Bi3+ in the composite sample [41]. In Fig. 3c of the O1s XPS spectrum, the peaks at binding energies of 529.04 eV and 530.81 eV were corresponding to the BiO bond of the [Bi2O2]2+ layer [42]. In Fig. 3d of the I3d XPS spectrum, the peaks at binding energies of 618.43 eV and 629.90 eV could be attributed to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, respectively [43]. In the C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3e), the peaks located at 283.7, 284.2, 285.3, 287.4 and 288. 2 eV were attributed to CC, CN, CN, CO, and CO [38,44], respectively. The contents of CO (4.31%) and CO (6.03%) bonds for BiOI/9%N-GQDs were lower than that of CO (12.37%) and CO (25.90%) bonds for N-GQDs (Fig. S1), which may be attributed to the strong interaction between O in the N-GQDs and Bi3+ in the BiOI. In Fig. 3f, the peaks at 396.63, 398.85 and 401.10 eV were assigned to pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphene N, respectively. The peak at 401.95 eV was attributed to the NH bonds [37]. The relative contents of N element within NH, pyrrole N, graphene N and pyridine N were 6.56%, 21.76%, 31.10% and 32.57%, respectively. The doping form of N atom was mainly pyridine N and graphite N. Additionally, Fig. S2 displayed the Raman spectra of BiOI and BiOI/x%N-GQDs composites. Two characteristic bands at around 87.5 and 130.5 cm−1 were observed and correspond to A1g and Eg internal stretching modes of Bi-I, respectively [45], which proved the existence of Bi-I. For BiOI/x%N-GQDs composites, the intensity of A1g and Eg peaks was weaker than those of pure BiOI under the same measurement condition, further indicating that there was a strong interaction between N-GQDs and BiOI [46].

SEM images of BiOI and BiOI/9%N-GQDs were shown in Fig. 4. From the Fig. 4a and b, it can be observed that the pure BiOI displayed an irregular micro flower assembled by nanosheets with the thickness ranging from about 8 to 18 nm. EDS spectrum of Fig. 4c displayed that BiOI contains three elements of Bi, O and I, and the contents were 34.03%, 22.79% and 43.10%, respectively. The elemental mapping image of Fig. 4d showed the three elements were evenly distributed. From the Fig. 4e and f, it can be seen that the BiOI/9%N-GQDs sample exhibited cuboid rod-like structures assembled by very thin nanoflakes with the thickness ranging from about 4 to 12 nm. The Fig. 4g and h clearly indicated that BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite contained C, N, O, Bi, and I elements, and the five elements exhibited a uniform distribution on the surface of BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite.

To confirm the distribution of BiOI and N-GQDs for BiOI/9%N-GQDs, we proceeded TEM and HRTEM of BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite. According to the TEM image of BiOI/9%N-GQDs in the Fig. 5a and b, the samples were assembled and interweaved by several thin nanoflakes. Fig. 5c showed BiOI nanoflakes were modified by N-GQDs. The HRTEM image of BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite in Fig. 5d displayed that the lattice space of 0.30 nm was in accordance with BiOI (102) lattice planes, while the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm was in accordance with N-GQDs (11
2 ¯
0) lattice planes. The above characterized results indicated that BiOI/N-GQDs composites were successfully fabricated by simple precipitation method.

MO was difficultly decomposed due to the existence of the azo structure. Only 2.5% of 20 mg/L MO was degraded under the irradiation of visible light (λ＞400 nm). Based on this, the photocatalytic properties of the prepared BiOI/x%N-GQDs (x = 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10) photocatalysts were firstly tested by degrading 20 mg/L MO under visible light. The equilibrium of adsorption and desorption of the catalysts towards MO can be achieved in the dark for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 6a, the adsorption efficiency and the degradation efficiency of the pure phase BiOI were 11.72% in the dark and 71.98% under visible light for 180 min, respectively. When the N-GQDs were modified on the BiOI materials, both absorption property and photocatalytic activity of all the composites were greatly improved. The concrete results were shown in the Fig. 6b. With the increase of N-GQDs content, the adsorption and photocatalytic efficiency of BiOI/x%N-GQDs firstly increased then decreased. In addition, the reaction kinetics data of BiOI/x%N-GQDs degrading MO could be calculated by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model [47]. Fig. 6c showed the first-order reaction kinetics diagram of the prepared photocatalysts. There were linear relationships between ln(C/C0) and irradiation time for the composited catalysts. Usually, the slope of the line was proportional to the degradation efficiency. The BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite showed the maximum rate constant of 12.09 × 10−3 min−1 among the prepared catalysts, indicating the photocatalytic property was the best. The result was in accordance with the degradation efficiency. Pure phase BiOI had been modified by N-GQDs to improve the photocatalytic activity. Among them, the adsorption and degradation properties of BiOI/9%N-GQDs were the most superior, and the values of BiOI/9%N-GQDs obtained 39.99% and 93.11%, respectively. The photocatalytic process of BiOI/9%N-GQDs degrading 20 mg/L MO was displayed in the Fig. 6d. The intensity of MO absorption peaks gradually decreased with the increasing of irradiation time, indicating that MO was fully mineralized.

To further explore the reason why BiOI/9%N-GQDs exhibited the best photocatalytic property, we measured and contrasted the light-absorbed ability and recombination situation of photo-generated carriers about the as-prepared catalysts. The results were shown in the Fig. 7. From the UV–vis DRS (Fig. 7a), it can be seen that these photocatalysts could adsorb the ultraviolet-visible light in the range from 200 nm to 650 nm, while the absorption intensity of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was stronger than that of other catalysts. The responding band gap curves of Fig. 7b and Table S1 showed the photocatalysts exhibited similar band gaps with 1.71–1.92 eV. The PL spectrum was used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance and could reflect the recombination states of photogenerated electrons and holes. The PL emission spectra of the prepared catalysts were obtained with a 315 nm laser as excitation source. The emission peaks of the samples were all around 470 nm, however the intensities of emission peaks were different. Generally, there was a positive relation between the intensity of emission peak and the recombination efficiency of photo-generated carriers. In order to clearly show the difference of the emission peak intensity among these catalysts, the intensity of emission peak at 470 nm was plotted in the bar graph of Fig. 7c. It can be observed from Fig. 7c that the emission peak intensity of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was the lowest. The data indicated that the recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers was the lowest, which meant the separation efficiency of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was the highest. Based on the above results, BiOI/9%N-GQDs possessed stronger light absorption ability and better separation efficiency of photo-generated carrier. It was concluded that the photo-current of BiOI/9%N-GQDs should be higher. As shown in Fig. 7d, it was true that the photo-current of BiOI/9%N-GQDs (0.53 μA) was much higher than that of BiOI (0.30 μA).

Based on the above results, the modification of N-GQDs enhanced the light-absorbed ability and the separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers. In particular, BiOI/9%N-GQDs possessed the suitable band gap of 1.92 eV. As far as we known, a narrower band gap needed less energy to excite photo-generated electrons and holes, but the generated electrons and holes more easily recombined for the narrower band gap. Therefore, a suitable band gap was necessary to high photocatalytic activity. In addition, the absorption role of target pollutants was the key of degradation process. The N-GQDs contained π-π conjugated structure, and nitrogen and oxygen atoms with strong electronegativity, which improved the absorption roles between BiOI/x%N-GQDs composite and target pollutants with conjugated structure through π-π interaction and electrostatic action.

In order to confirm the change in the internal band structure, we also measured the XPS valence spectra. The results were shown in Fig. S3. From Fig. S3, the corresponding energies of valence band (EVB) of BiOI and BiOI/9%N-GQDs were measured to be 0.95 and 1.25 eV, respectively. Then, the EVB of the corresponding standard hydrogen electrode (EVB, NHE) can be calculated based on the following formula: EVB, NHE = φ + EVB − 4.44, where φ is the work function of the instrument (4.69 eV) [48]. Therefore, the EVB, NHE of BiOI and BiOI/9%N-GQDs was 1.20 and 1.50 eV, respectively. From the optical absorption spectrum (Fig. 7b), the band gaps (Eg) of BiOI and BiOI/9%N-GQDs were determined to be 1.71 and 1.92 eV, respectively. Thus, the energy of conduction band (CB) was determined to be −0.51 and −0.42 eV according to the formula ECB = EVB − Eg. Therefore, the modification of N-GQDs enhanced the redox ability of BiOI/9%N-GQDs. Based on the above characterization and analysis, the enhanced photocatalytic property of BiOI/9%N-GQDs could be attributed to the good absorption of target pollutant, the strong light-absorbed ability, the lower recombination rate of carriers, the suitable band gap and the strong redox ability.

In the process of photocatalytical removing pollutants, the photocatalyst BiOI/9%N-GQDs could absorb 400–646 nm visible light, and then the electrons were excited from VB with 1.50 eV to CB with −0.42 eV, leaving holes in the VB and forming electron-hole pairs. The produced electrons could react with oxygen to form O2–·. O2–· not only reduced as-generated H2O2 to HO· but also could directly degrade the absorbed pollutants. The holes in the VB quickly transferred to the surface of catalyst, which not only oxidized OH groups to HO· but also could directly degrade the absorbed pollutants. To clarify the role of three active free radicals (h+, O2–· and HO·) during the process of BiOI/9% N-GQDs degrading MO, the trapping experiments of active species were carried out. The ammonium oxalate (AO), isopropanol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ) could be selected as scavengers for holes (h+), hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide radical (·O2−), respectively. As shown in Fig. 8a, the photodegradation efficiency of BiOI/9% N-GQDs in the presence of AO, BQ, and IPA was 2.30%, 44.54% and 83.06%, respectively, while the photodegradation efficiency of BiOI/9%N-GQDs without any scavengers was 93.11%. These data indicated that the role of the active species for MO photodegradation was as follows: h+ >> O2–· >HO·.

To further examine the formation of ·OH, PL spectral changes of BiOI/9%N-GQDs were measured in the presence of terephthalic acid in NaOH solution under visible light [49]. The results were shown in Fig. 8b, the intensity of the fluorescence emission peak near 420 nm slowly increased and then decreased with the increasing of irradiation time, indicating that ·OH could produce and gradually increase, then decrease after 80 min irradiation. However, the degradation efficiency of BiOI/9%N-GQDs always increased with the increasing of irradiation time. Therefore, ·OH was not the main active species during the degradation of MO, which was consistent with the results of free radical trapping experiments. The possible photocatalytic mechanism was shown in the Fig. 9.

BiOI/9%N-GQDs exhibited an excellent photocatalytic property towards MO. To further investigate the photocatalytic property of BiOI/9%N-GQDs, we selected some representative simulated pollutants with different conjugated systems, such as RhB, MB and BPA, and high valence inorganic material, such as Cr(Ⅵ). The degradation processes were shown in Fig. 10. For RhB (20 mg/L) in Fig. 10a, the adsorption efficiency was approximately 50%, and the degradation efficiency of RhB was 98.24% at 180 min under visible light irradiation. For MB (20 mg/L) in Fig. 10b, the degradation efficiency obtained 72.36% at 180 min under visible light. As shown in Fig. 10c, the degradation efficiency of BiOI/9%N-GQDs towards 20 mg/L BPA was 66.34% at 180 min. For 15 mg/L heavy metal Cr(Ⅵ) in the Fig. 10d, the degradation efficiency was 95.19% at 150 min. As shown in Table S2, the turnover frequency of BiOI/9%N-GQDs catalyst was higher than those of the recently reported BiOI composite photocatalysts. These results indicated that BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite possessed remarkable visible light catalytic activity.

BiOI/x%N-GQDs (x = 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10) photocatalysts were successfully prepared by facial precipitation method using tetrapropylammonium iodide as iodide source. Several characterized results exhibited N-GQDs were evenly in-situ modified on the surface of BiOI. Through degrading 20 mg/L MO, BiOI/9%N-GQDs possessed the best degradation property and the degradation efficiency obtained 93.11% at 180 min under visible light among as prepared samples. During the process of degrading MO, the active species h+ played a main role. The remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity of BiOI/9%N-GQDs was mainly due to the good absorption of target pollutant, the strong light-absorbed ability, the lower recombination rate of carriers, the suitable band gap (1.92 eV) and the strong redox ability, because of decorated N-GQDs. Moreover, BiOI/9%N-GQDs could degrade several pollutants, such as RhB and MB with a large conjugated system, BPA with a small conjugated system, and high valence inorganic ions Cr(Ⅵ). The photocatalytic activity was higher than those of the recently reported similar catalytic system. Hence, the prepared BiOI/9%N-GQDs composite should have better practical application in the treatment of organic pollutants and inorganic ions. The present study using N-GQDs as an effective pollutants-absorbed, light-harvesting and electron-transmission embellishments could be extended to design other visible light-driven photocatalysts.

Xueling Zhu: Prepared the samples, tested and analyzed the photocatalytic properties, wrote and revised the manuscript. Xiya Guo: Designed the experimental scheme, Prepared the samples, tested and analyzed the photocatalytic properties. Jinling Song: Determined the experimental scheme, analyzed and discussed the experimental. results, revised and checked the manuscript and the other relative files. Pei Han: Analyzed the structure of the prepared samples. Guoxiang Xin: Analyzed the structure of the prepared samples. Ruifen Wang: Analyzed and discussed the photocatalytic mechanism.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are so grateful for the supports from the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia (Nos. 2019MS02023, 2021MS05011, 2020ZD17), the Innovation Foundation of Inner Mongolia University of Science and Technology (No. 2018YQL01) and the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21407084).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127841.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
